one_line_takeaway: A survey/position paper arguing that XAI's unique safety value in end-to-end (vs. modular) autonomous driving is data-centric failure diagnosis, backed by a small qualitative demo that off-the-shelf video-QA explainers hallucinate under misleading questions; it is an AV-safety/XAI paper, not an AI-security contribution.
key_finding: In the authors' own Experiment 1, Video-LLaVA gives correct action + correct explanation on the 3 BDD-A conventional-question scenes (Scenarios 1-3) but is easily broken by deliberate questions on SHIFT: it hallucinates a red traffic light where none exists (Scenario 4), claims a left turn 'to avoid oncoming traffic' when the vehicle actually turns right (Scenario 5, correct action / wrong explanation), and, when the ego vehicle runs a red light, wrongly reports the light turned green (Scenario 6, unsafe action / wrong explanation). The authors conclude transformer-based QA models are currently unreliable as real-time HMI explainers and should 'fail safe' (decline to explain) rather than emit convincing but fictitious explanations. The paper's overarching finding: for end-to-end driving the unique safety payoff of XAI is surfacing training-data gaps and guiding data augmentation, a diagnostic route unavailable to per-module attribution in modular pipelines.
core_contribution: A survey/position paper (accepted 2025, arXiv:2403.12176v5) that examines the SAFETY implications of explainable AI (XAI) in END-TO-END autonomous driving, a gap the authors argue prior XAI-for-AV work (which targets trust/transparency and mostly covers modular pipelines) has left unexplored. Its own contributions are threefold: (1) analytical case studies showing how real-time and retrospective explanations enhance end-to-end driving safety (real-time safety monitoring, failure detection, the 'Molly problem' of witnessless AV collisions analyzed over a proposed 3-phase before/at/after-hitting scheme, and spatiotemporal object-centric accident analysis); (2) a small qualitative experiment (Experiment 1) with a video-question-answering explainer; and (3) a synthesizing framework distinguishing end-to-end from modular driving and naming five safety values of explanations ('Explain to control / enhance / defend / adapt / comply'). The paper's distinctive thesis is that for end-to-end (monolithic sensor-to-control) models the main safety lever of explanations is a DATA perspective: explanations expose latent training-data patterns behind failures and guide targeted data augmentation, whereas modular pipelines instead attribute failures to a specific module.
threat_model: No formal threat model; this is not a security paper. Adversarial content appears only illustratively: (a) the cited McAfee 'model hacking' demo where a sticker makes a 35-mph sign read as 85-mph on a Tesla ADAS, used to motivate causal explanations; and (b) the authors' own observation that a Video-LLaVA explainer can be steered by deliberately misleading ('adversarial') passenger questions into fabricating scene facts. These motivate an 'Explain to defend' value and future work on human-adversarial-example training, but the paper defines no attacker capabilities, assets, or security guarantees.
limitations: By the authors' own admission the study is scenario-based within a narrow scope and does not address explanation-anomaly detection, robustness/time constraints of explanations, or confidence/uncertainty-aware explanations. Experiment 1 is qualitative, tiny (6 cherry-picked clips), single-model, with no baselines, metrics, statistical analysis, or human evaluation, so its failure examples are anecdotal rather than measured. Experiment 2's numbers are not the authors' own results. Much of the persuasive quantitative material (the Molly-problem survey, the McAfee speed-sign hack, feature-importance rankings) is drawn from cited work and should not be read as this paper's empirical contribution. For an AI-SECURITY knowledge graph this record is off-topic: it is a vehicular-safety / XAI position paper (the 'LLM Safety Alignment' cluster label is a misfit), touching adversarial ML only tangentially.

datasets_used: SHIFT (driving simulation dataset) — source of Scenarios 4-6 in the authors' own Experiment 1
BDD-A / Berkeley DeepDrive Attention (real-world driving dataset) — source of Scenarios 1-3 in Experiment 1
MM-AU (Multi-Modal Accident Video Understanding, Fang et al.) — CITED only, used to illustrate object-centric accident analysis, not run by the authors
quantitative_results: Experiment 1 (authors' own): 6 driving clips, lengths 4-12 s; 3/6 scenes (BDD-A Scenarios 1-3) yield correct-action/correct-explanation; Scenarios 4, 5, 6 (SHIFT) each expose a distinct failure (hallucinated red light; right turn mislabeled as left; red-light run explained as 'light turned green')
Explainer model: single off-the-shelf Video-LLaVA multimodal transformer, used plug-and-play; no baseline compared

CITED (not authors' own) — Cui et al. SHAP study: 5-D state [d, egovx, egovy, frontvx, frontvy], 3 actions [Slower, Idle, Faster]; egovx, d, frontvx most influential, egovy and frontvy deemed removable

CITED (not authors' own) — Molly-problem survey [128], 296 respondents aged 18-73: 75% favored traveling in AVs; Table IV: 97% expected the AI to be aware of the collision, 94% expected it to stop at the collision area, 97% expected it to signal a hazard to road users
CITED (not authors' own) — McAfee 'model hacking': a sticker altered a 35-mph sign, read as 85-mph by a Tesla Model S ADAS heads-up display
CITED (not authors' own) — takeover timing: Huang & Pitts contrast 4 s vs 7 s lead time (shorter = faster but poorer-quality takeover); AV reaction time cited as ~100 ms
baselines_compared: None — the authors' Experiment 1 is a qualitative single-model (Video-LLaVA) demonstration with no baseline models, quantitative metrics, or benchmark comparison
